prompt:
Subscription incident from the billing desk. A combined plan-and-seat update commits correctly, but the customer receives two `subscription updated` messages: the first describes the new plan with the old seat count, and the second has the final values. A plan-only or seat-only update sends one message. Separate successful updates are supposed to send separately.

The small app models the production route: an update service saves each changed field, model callbacks defer a job, the queue runs that job, and the mailer records the delivery. Please trace the callback chain and make the transaction contract true in one place: for a given subscription and side effect, a successful transaction publishes once using the latest committed state. Different subscriptions and different callback keys must not collapse together, later transactions must still publish, and rollback must publish nothing and restore the record. Do not add independent sent-flags to the service, job, and mailer or globally suppress retries. Existing validation, version increments, queue behavior, and mail payloads stay intact.

Run `ruby test_callback_chain.rb`. It uses only in-memory fakes and the test file is protected.

full_distill.primary_skill: trace subscription saves through model callbacks, deferred jobs, and mailers to deduplicate side effects at the transaction commit boundary
full_distill.baseline_evidence: ruby test_callback_chain.rb fails because one combined update enqueues and delivers two messages, including an intermediate stale-seat payload
full_distill.success_contract: each successful transaction runs the latest callback once per logical key while preserving distinct keys, subscriptions, later transactions, rollback atomicity, versions, jobs, and mail payloads
adversarial_factors: a protected test registers duplicate callbacks directly on the unit of work, so service-local or mailer-local guards do not establish transaction semantics
the expected delivery contains the final plan, seats, and version, so keeping the first callback rather than the latest callback is observably wrong
distinct callback keys and two subscription IDs share one transaction, rejecting a blanket one-callback-per-transaction suppression
two separate transactions must deliver twice, rejecting process-global or queue-global deduplication
a validation failure after the first save must restore the record and enqueue nothing, so early publication and exception swallowing both fail
